epigraph.text:
For I know the plans I have for you, declares the 
Lord, plans to prosper you and not to harm you, plans to give you 
hope and a future.

content.text:
In Chapter 5, we encountered antiderivatives as the reverse of 
differentiation: given $f$, find an $F$ such that $F' = f$. We treated 
them as a tool for solving differential equations and recovering position 
from velocity. That was useful—but it was only the beginning. In this 
chapter, we will discover that antiderivatives are connected to something 
much deeper: the problem of computing *area*. The link between 
these two seemingly unrelated ideas—reversing derivatives and measuring 
area—is the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, the crown jewel of the 
subject.

But first, let us sharpen our antidifferentiation skills. We will need 
them.
The Antiderivative Revisited
Recall the core idea.

content.title: Antiderivative
content.content:
A function $F$ is an **antiderivative** of $f$ 
on an interval $I$ if
\[
 F'(x) = f(x) \quad\text{for every } x \in I.
\]

content.text:
For instance, $F(x) = x^3$ is an antiderivative of $f(x) = 3x^2$ because 
$\deriv{}{x}(x^3) = 3x^2$. But so is $G(x) = x^3 + 5$, and 
$H(x) = x^3 - \pi$. In fact, *any* function of the form $x^3 + C$ 
is an antiderivative of $3x^2$. Are there others?

No. If $F$ and $G$ are both antiderivatives of $f$ on an interval, then 
$(F - G)' = f - f = 0$ on that interval. By a consequence of the Mean 
Value Theorem, any function whose derivative is identically zero on an 
interval must be constant. Therefore $F(x) - G(x) = C$ for some 
constant $C$, and every antiderivative of $f$ has the form $F(x) + C$.

content.title: General Antiderivative
content.content:
If $F$ is any one antiderivative of $f$ on an interval $I$, then the 
**most general antiderivative** of $f$ on $I$ is
\[
 F(x) + C, \qquad C \in \R,
\]
where $C$ is an arbitrary constant called the 
**constant of integration**.
content.label: Theorem

content.text: Indefinite Integral Notation

We write the general antiderivative of $f$ using the 
**indefinite integral** notation:
\[
 \int f(x)\dx = F(x) + C,
\]
where $F'(x) = f(x)$. The elongated "S" symbol $\int$ was introduced 
by Leibniz—it stands for *summa*, the Latin word for "sum." 
(We will see why this is perfect when we study Riemann sums in 
Section [sec:riemann-sums].) The function $f(x)$ is called the 
**integrand**, and $\dx$ indicates the variable of integration.

content.title: The constant $C$ is not optional
content.content:
Writing $\displaystyle\int 2x\dx = x^2$ without the "${}+ C$" is 
*wrong*. There are infinitely many antiderivatives of $2x$, and 
they differ by a constant. Always include $C$ unless you are given an 
initial condition that determines its value.

content.text: Basic Antiderivative Rules

Every differentiation rule we learned gives us an antidifferentiation 
rule—we simply read the table backwards. If $\deriv{}{x}[\sin x] = 
\cos x$, then $\int \cos x\dx = \sin x + C$.

content.title: Power Rule for Antiderivatives
content.content:
For any real number $n \neq -1$:
\[
 \int x^n \dx = \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} + C.
\]
When $n = -1$, we have $\displaystyle\int x^{-1}\dx = \int \frac{1}{x}\dx 
= \ln\abs{x} + C$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Differentiate the right-hand side:
\[
 \deriv{}{x}\left[\frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}\right] 
 = \frac{1}{n+1}\cdot (n+1)\, x^{n} = x^n. \qedhere
\]

content.text:
Notice the restriction $n \neq -1$: if $n = -1$ the formula 
$\frac{x^{0}}{0}$ is undefined. The natural logarithm fills that gap.

content.title: Power rule antiderivatives
content.solution:
1. $\displaystyle\int x^7\dx = \frac{x^8}{8} + C.$

2. Rewrite as $x^{-4}$:
 $\displaystyle\int x^{-4}\dx = \frac{x^{-3}}{-3} + C 
 = -\frac{1}{3x^3} + C.$

3. Rewrite as $x^{1/2}$:
 $\displaystyle\int x^{1/2}\dx = \frac{x^{3/2}}{3/2} + C 
 = \frac{2}{3}\,x^{3/2} + C.$

content.text: Two linearity properties make antidifferentiation much easier.

content.title: Linearity of the Indefinite Integral
content.content:
If $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$ and $G$ is an antiderivative of $g$, 
and $c$ is any constant, then:

1. **Constant Multiple Rule:** 
 $\displaystyle\int c\, f(x)\dx = c\int f(x)\dx$

2. **Sum/Difference Rule:** 
 $\displaystyle\int \bigl[f(x) \pm g(x)\bigr]\dx 
 = \int f(x)\dx \pm \int g(x)\dx$
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
These follow directly from the corresponding rules for derivatives. Armed 
with the power rule and linearity, we can antidifferentiate any polynomial.

content.title: Antiderivative of a polynomial
content.solution:
$$
 \int(6x^3 - 4x + 5)\dx 
 &= 6\cdot\frac{x^4}{4} - 4\cdot\frac{x^2}{2} + 5x + C \\
 &= \frac{3x^4}{2} - 2x^2 + 5x + C.
$$
We can verify by differentiating: 
$\deriv{}{x}\!\left[\frac{3x^4}{2} - 2x^2 + 5x + C\right] 
= 6x^3 - 4x + 5$.

content.text: Trigonometric and Exponential Antiderivatives

Reading our differentiation tables in reverse, we obtain the following 
antiderivatives.

Every entry in this table should be verified by differentiating the 
right-hand column. If $F'(x) = f(x)$, the formula is correct.

content.title: Trigonometric antiderivatives
content.problem:
Find each antiderivative.

1. $\displaystyle\int (3\sin x + 2\cos x)\dx$

2. $\displaystyle\int \sec x(\sec x + \tan x)\dx$
content.solution:
1. By linearity and the table:
 \[
 \int (3\sin x + 2\cos x)\dx = -3\cos x + 2\sin x + C.
 \]

2. Expand the product:
 \[
 \int(\sec^2 x + \sec x\tan x)\dx = \tan x + \sec x + C.
 \]

content.title: Rewriting before integrating
content.solution:
We cannot integrate a quotient term-by-term in general, but here we can 
simplify by dividing each term in the numerator by $x^2$:
\[
 \frac{x^3 + 2x^2 - 1}{x^2} = x + 2 - x^{-2}.
\]
Now integrate:
\[
 \int\bigl(x + 2 - x^{-2}\bigr)\dx 
 = \frac{x^2}{2} + 2x + \frac{1}{x} + C.
\]

content.title: No product or quotient rule for integrals
content.content:
There is no rule that says $\int f(x)\,g(x)\dx = \int f(x)\dx \cdot 
\int g(x)\dx$. This is *false* in general. Integration is harder 
than differentiation precisely because we lack such simple rules. We 
will develop techniques—substitution in Section [sec:substitution], 
and more in Chapter 8—to handle products and compositions.

content.text: Initial Value Problems

The constant $C$ in the general antiderivative represents a whole 
*family* of functions—infinitely many curves, each one a vertical 
shift of the others. An **initial condition** picks out a single 
member of this family.

content.title: Initial Value Problem
content.content:
An **initial value problem** (IVP) asks 
us to find a function $y = f(x)$ satisfying
\[
 f'(x) = g(x), \qquad f(x_0) = y_0,
\]
where $g$ is a given function and $(x_0, y_0)$ is a specified point. The 
condition $f(x_0) = y_0$ is the **initial condition**.

content.text:
The strategy is straightforward: find the general antiderivative, then 
use the initial condition to determine $C$.

content.title: Finding a particular antiderivative
content.solution:
The general antiderivative is
\[
 f(x) = \int(4x^3 - 6x + 1)\dx = x^4 - 3x^2 + x + C.
\]
Apply the initial condition $f(1) = 3$:
\[
 1 - 3 + 1 + C = 3 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C = 4.
\]
Therefore $f(x) = x^4 - 3x^2 + x + 4$.

content.title: Position from velocity
content.problem:
A particle moves along a straight line with velocity 
$v(t) = 3t^2 - 10t + 4$ meters per second. Its position at time $t = 0$ 
is $s(0) = 2$ meters. Find its position function $s(t)$.
content.solution:
Since $s'(t) = v(t)$, we antidifferentiate:
\[
 s(t) = \int(3t^2 - 10t + 4)\dt = t^3 - 5t^2 + 4t + C.
\]
The initial condition $s(0) = 2$ gives $C = 2$, so
\[
 s(t) = t^3 - 5t^2 + 4t + 2.
\]

content.title: Velocity from acceleration, then position
content.problem:
A ball is thrown upward from a height of $48$ feet with an initial velocity 
of $32$ feet per second. The acceleration due to gravity is 
$a(t) = -32\;\text{ft/s}^2$. Find the velocity function $v(t)$ and the 
position function $s(t)$.
content.solution:
**Step 1: Find velocity.** 
Since $v'(t) = a(t) = -32$:
\[
 v(t) = \int(-32)\dt = -32t + C_1.
\]
The initial condition $v(0) = 32$ gives $C_1 = 32$, so 
$v(t) = -32t + 32$.

**Step 2: Find position.** 
Since $s'(t) = v(t) = -32t + 32$:
\[
 s(t) = \int(-32t + 32)\dt = -16t^2 + 32t + C_2.
\]
The initial condition $s(0) = 48$ gives $C_2 = 48$, so
\[
 s(t) = -16t^2 + 32t + 48.
\]
The ball hits the ground when $s(t) = 0$: solving 
$-16t^2 + 32t + 48 = 0$ gives $t^2 - 2t - 3 = 0$, so 
$(t-3)(t+1) = 0$ and $t = 3$ seconds (discarding $t = -1$).

content.title: Second-order initial value problem
content.solution:
**Step 1:** Antidifferentiate once to find $f'$:
\[
 f'(x) = \int(12x - 6)\dx = 6x^2 - 6x + C_1.
\]
From $f'(0) = 4$: $C_1 = 4$, so $f'(x) = 6x^2 - 6x + 4$.

**Step 2:** Antidifferentiate again:
\[
 f(x) = \int(6x^2 - 6x + 4)\dx = 2x^3 - 3x^2 + 4x + C_2.
\]
From $f(0) = 1$: $C_2 = 1$, so $f(x) = 2x^3 - 3x^2 + 4x + 1$.

content.text: A Glimpse Ahead

We now have the tools to reverse any derivative we can recognize. But a 
natural question arises: *why* is antidifferentiation important 
beyond recovering position from velocity?

In the next section, we will tackle a problem that seems completely 
unrelated: *How do you find the area under a curve?* We will 
attack it by dividing the region into rectangles and adding up their 
areas—a process that appears to have nothing to do with 
antiderivatives. And yet, as we shall discover in 
Section [sec:ftc], the two problems are deeply, beautifully, 
and unexpectedly the same.

content.caption: The antiderivatives of $f(x) = 3x^2$ form a family of cubic curves $F(x) = x^3 + C$. Each curve has the same slope at any $x$-value, differing only by a vertical shift.
content.alt: A coordinate plane showing a family of cubic curves, all with the same shape but shifted vertically. Five curves are drawn, each representing a different antiderivative of f of x equals 3x squared. From bottom to top, they are labeled x cubed minus 2, x cubed minus 1, x cubed, x cubed plus 1, and x cubed plus 2. All curves have the same slope at any given x-value because they share the same derivative. Short parallel tangent line segments at x equals 1 demonstrate this: each curve has slope 3 at that point. A bracket on the right indicates that all these curves are written as F of x equals x cubed plus C, where C is the constant of integration.

marginNotes.text:
What is has already been, and what will 
be has been before; and God will call the past to account.

The heavens declare the glory of God; the 
skies proclaim the work of his hands. Day after day they pour forth 
speech; night after night they reveal knowledge.

And we know that in all things God works 
for the good of those who love him, who have been called according to 
his purpose.

marginNotes.author: Gottfried Leibniz
marginNotes.text:
It is unworthy of excellent men to lose 
hours like slaves in the labor of calculation which could be safely 
relegated to anyone else if machines were used.

marginNotes.author: Isaac Barrow
marginNotes.text:
The whole of mathematics consists in the 
organization of a series of aids to the imagination in the process 
of reasoning.
